articles.title: Turning an Old Farm into a Peat Research Center
articles.summary: An old dairy farm in Somerset, UK, might become a special research center for studying peat bogs. Peat bogs are wet, swampy areas that store a lot of carbon, which helps fight climate change. The Somerset Wildlife Trust wants to change the farm buildings at Honeygar Farm into a place where scientists can learn how to restore these important wetlands. This research could help with problems like climate change and clean water. The farm is on the Somerset Levels and Moors, one of the few places in the UK with deep lowland peat. The trust bought the farm in 2021 and has been letting nature recover there. They plan to add public paths and viewing areas so people can visit and learn too. The local government will decide if the plans can go ahead.

articles.title: A Cool New Way to Make Medicines with Light
articles.summary: Scientists at Cambridge University made a big discovery by accident! They found a way to use light from an LED lamp to change drug molecules, which are tiny parts that make medicines work. Usually, making medicines needs strong chemicals that can be harmful and take a long time. But with this new method, they can make changes to the molecules later in the process, like adding a small piece without breaking everything apart. This helps make drug development faster, safer, and better for the environment because it uses less energy and creates less waste. The light triggers a reaction that forms important bonds, called carbon-carbon bonds, which are like the building blocks of many things, including medicines. This means scientists can test new ideas for drugs more quickly and easily.

articles.title: Ancient DNA Switches Found in Plants
articles.summary: Scientists have discovered ancient DNA switches in plants that are over 400 million years old. These switches, called conserved non-coding sequences (CNSs), help control when genes turn on or off in plants. Researchers used a new tool called Conservatory to find more than 2.3 million of these switches by comparing the DNA of 314 plant genomes from 284 species. They found that some switches are very old, dating back to before flowering plants existed. The study shows that these switches are important for plant growth and development. Scientists can now use this information to learn more about how plants evolve and to help improve crops like food plants. This discovery helps solve a long-standing puzzle about whether such DNA switches stay the same in plants over millions of years.

articles.title: Our Sun's Big Move with Star Friends
articles.summary: Scientists think our Sun might have moved away from the center of the Milky Way galaxy a long time ago with thousands of other stars that are just like it. They used a special satellite called Gaia to look at many stars and found that the Sun and these twin stars are about 4 to 6 billion years old and are in similar places in space. This suggests they all traveled together. The Milky Way has a bar shape in the middle that usually stops stars from moving out, but maybe it was still forming back then. Moving to a quieter part of the galaxy helped make Earth a safe place for life to grow.

articles.title: Why AIs Have Trouble with Some Games
articles.summary: AIs like AlphaGo are great at games like chess and Go, but they struggle with other games. Researchers found that AIs get confused by games like Nim, where players take turns removing matchsticks from a pyramid-shaped board. In Nim, you can figure out who is winning by using a special math function. The AI learns by playing itself, but for Nim, this method doesn't work well because the game has simple rules but tricky math. This helps scientists understand AI mistakes and make them better at more games in the future.

articles.title: Magnetars and Super Bright Star Explosions
articles.summary: Some stars explode in huge bursts called superluminous supernovae, which are among the brightest things in space. Scientists like Joseph Farah have been trying to figure out why they are so powerful. They think magnetars, which are super strong spinning neutron stars, might be the reason. Magnetars are born when a star collapses and spin very fast, releasing energy that lights up the explosion. But scientists noticed a problem: the light from these explosions doesn't fade smoothly; it has bumps and wiggles over months. A new discovery helped solve this. In 2024, scientists saw a supernova called SN 2024afav that had bumps in its light, and the time between bumps got shorter, like a chirping sound. They predicted when the next bumps would happen and were right. This showed that the old magnetar idea didn't fully explain things. Farah's team came up with a new idea using frame-dragging, where a spinning magnetar drags spacetime around it, causing the bumps. This matches what we see and helps us understand these amazing explosions better.
